speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: March the 8th, 1995. The survivor is Lola Orzech, interviewer Carol Stulberg, North Hollywood, USA, and it will be in English. Just say-- you announced the names already. OK, let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm Carol Stulberg, and it's Wednesday March the 8th, 1995. I have the privilege of interviewing Lola Orzech. Mrs. Orzech, can you please tell me when and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in August 1, 1925 in Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola Feldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Matilda Feldman. And my father was Leon Markheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father's name is Leon Markheim, and your mother was Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you go by the name of Feldman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but because they didn't have the civil wedding, just the Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And what were the names of your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the oldest one was Regina, and then was Herman, and then was Ernestina. Then it was one sister who passed away while she was an infant. Then it was me, Lola. And then it was Dora and Marilke.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living to support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about your life in Krakow before the war-- your childhood, what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a very nice, traditional home. And we live on the Kazimierz, that was the Jewish section. And the address was Krakowska 37. And I was 14 years old when the war broke out. And we had a very nice life until the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your life. What were the holidays like, and school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traditional. Well, my father was not going every day to the-- to the temple, just on Saturdays and high holidays. But the home was Kosher. Every Friday night there were candles on the table and traditional dinner. And-- but my father was a little bit modern in a way. My parents used to go to theater, to the movie. And I was the middle child. I was always like in between the-- in between those two. My older sisters, they were working already. My brother was working. But I was just still at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: School? Well, I finished seventh grade-- seven years of grade school. Like here, it's maybe some junior high. And I lived right across the school. And I enjoyed it. I enjoyed it very much. We had a very nice--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live mostly in a Jewish section? Or did you live with Christians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was Jewish section. The name was the Kazi-- not [? Kazimierzo, ?] Kazimierz. And I have girlfriends, lot of girlfriends and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember experiencing any anti-Semitism or being aware of it all before the war? Well,

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going together-- it was mixed at school, the Gentile. And I remember, I didn't have no-- no problems, because my father was not dressed up like all the Orthodox. Although we lived in a home what was lots of other people living, and they all were Orthodox. Us and another couple what that wasn't. And-- and I remember at school, when came religion-- to the-- we had one hour of religion twice, I think, a week. And the Jewish kids went to a different room, and the Gentile to the other. Saturday school was open. None of us from the Jewish kids went. And it was anti-Semitism, but I didn't pay so much, you know. Of course, my friends were only Jewish. I-- I did not associate with any-- any other. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you remember as being the first signs of trouble, right before the war? Do you remember anything before the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we knew that that's coming. And I remember, like, Jabotinsky came, and there was one house was a big space there, and he was talking. And he was telling people that the situation is bad and we should leave. But who-- we just listened, and who said, who wants to leave and-- and leave everything. Where are we going to go? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember from the occupation? What were your first memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when the war broke out? Well, first there was bombings. And then we were running to the cellar. I remember my grandmother came to stay with us. And my-- when they start to bomb, my oldest sister was-- she said, let's go quick, quick, quick. And my grandmother didn't want to go. And she was-- told her, if grandma doesn't want to go, don't go. We are young. We want to live. And then we-- we-- oh yeah, I remember also it started-- when-- when they came in, the Germans, to the-- I remember exactly the-- the night that they came into the-- to the town. And it started to be hard. We couldn't get enough bread. I remember getting up 5:00 in the morning to go to-- we were lucky, because right next to our home was a bakery. So we were going just out-- out of our door, and we stayed in the line. So every one of us got up to be in the line to get the loaf of-- loaf of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it came-- pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I'm sorry to interrupt you, but I-- I wanted to just ask you, go back a little bit and tell me what you remember when the Germans came in. What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were scared. We didn't know what to-- in the beginning, we didn't realize what-- what happened. We used to-- I used to live there, where my school was a big space there, and they were stationed there. And they were there shaving and, you know, themselves, and eating, and laughing, and-- and we were looking at them like-- we were scared. We didn't know what to-- one thing I remember when the war broke out, immediately my father send my brother quick to the bank to take out what-- whatever it was. His head was working. Whatever it was-- it was there. And then there start to be stories like they were catching people on the street to work and-- with the brooms on the streets, you know, to sweep out the-- the streets. They were catching those Orthodox Jews and-- and cutting off, right there on the street, the payot and-- and the-- the beards. And it was a scary scary, scary thing. And well, right away there was shortage of food and shortage of everything. And it was kind-- but it wasn't so bad, because we were still together. Then came a time they say everybody has to go to ghetto. And my parents were afraid. They didn't want to go to ghetto. So we went outskirts to Kocmyrzow. It was maybe a half a hour with the-- with the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year was this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- well, the war broke out in '39. And that was in '40, '41, something like that. And my parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stop just a minute please? OK, so you were saying that you left the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father, he went to Kocmyrzow, and they rented a-- it was a-- a room with a tiny kitchen. And we took our stuff, and whatever we had, and we went there. My brother went to ghetto. Oh, at that time, both of my sisters got married. Yeah. My Erna, the-- the third one in the row, she got married in '40. Then, later on, my oldest sister got married too. She married a fellow, what he came from-- like, they were-- first, they sent the-- all the German people from Germany, they sent to Krakow. And we all-- my older sister did not go to first to ghetto-- to-- to ghetto either, just with us. But then later on she went back to-- to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so who was with you when you left the city? Which--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, and-- and the three of us that's left at home. I mean, because my brother stayed in-- in ghetto. And over there, we-- we had some savings, so that's what we lived on. And besides that, my father used to travel on a train to ghetto. He-- at that time, we had to wear the armbands. So he put like a-- like a scarf, a plaid, over the armband, and he was traveling. At that time, Jewish people, we-- we couldn't anymore, you know, travel on the train, or-- so he put this scarf over-- over his band, and he traveled to ghetto. He was bringing some merchandise like fabrics and thread and shoes for the kids, and materials for aprons, for the [? pharmacist. ?] And then he was going to the farm, going from home to home, and selling them this. And for that he got flour, you know, meat, butter, eggs. And that's how we lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, in '42, beginning of '42, came that everybody from that place has to go to Wieliczka. So what do you do? You have to obey. So the-- the farmer what we lived there, he took a horse and a buggy and we put in still few things, and we all went to Wieliczka. Wieliczka-- at that time, my older sister was with us, all my-- Erna, my other sister, she lived that time in Kocmyrzow too. She didn't-- but she lived separate with her husband, and-- and-- and she had already a baby. '94 this year had that little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, we all went, and they put us over there, I remember, in a big, big, hall. And we didn't know what's waiting for us. And we were waiting. All of a sudden, after the third day, we see my brother is coming. And he says, I'm looking all over for you for a couple hours. Don't ask any questions, just come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all had prepared a little bundles, you know, with our personal things, but there was still-- we took some furniture, some other-- other things. So my father says, he didn't realize. And he says, who's going to-- what's going to happen to the things? So my oldest sister says, I will stay with the things. And when we came to the place, my brother hired-- he was in ghetto and he was doing business, and he knew some Germans. He was going out from the ghetto. He was smuggling in food. He really was, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyhow, he-- my brother gave whatever he had to a German guy, and they came with a-- with a big truck, and they pretended that they are bringing people to work to airport to Krakow. And by the time that we came to that truck, other people were pushing, to the German who was waiting, their diamonds and money. And when we came to the truck, there was-- the truck was almost full. Well, there was space for my mother, and-- and my sister with the baby on her arms, and my father. And me, my brother had to pick it up and throw me under people like a sack of potatoes. I lost my-- my shoes. And the same German who took the money from him to come, was hitting him. That was made specially to pretend that-- you know. And they smuggled us into the ghetto. No, at that time, we had a hard time to find a place, but my brother found a-- a little place for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, what do you do? There was at that time the ghetto A and ghetto B. A were the people what they have are working. You need some papers. I think they called it Kenkarta? I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, and we went to ghetto B, because my mother was not so healthy and she couldn't-- she couldn't work. And we didn't had any-- any work. My father got a job outskirts of Krakow, taking care of horses. And he was there. He didn't come home for night. He was there sleeping. And me and my older sister, Erna, she was bringing the baby to my mother. We were going to the-- they call it the Arbeitsamt. We were getting up 5:00 in the morning to go, maybe they would give us some kind of a-- a job to get some papers, because only people who were working could get-- be legally in ghetto. Anyhow, so that's-- that time when they gave me the job, by in [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That was for Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year it was? Winter? Summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. It was summer. It was summer, maybe even spring. May, June, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So me and-- and my sister, the one who had the baby, they sent her to Kabel to another factory. My youngest sister, Dora, I was waking her up in the morning and telling her to come on get up-- she was only two, three years younger than me-- come on, let's get up. She says, I'm not getting up. I'm not working for Germans. And she didn't want to go. How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the war broke out I was 14. That was in '39. So I was 16? 17? Well anyhow, so still from ghetto, I was going back and forth with a group, you know, we-- with a German, back and forth, working shifts day night-- one week days, one week nights. 12 hours, 12 hours a-- a shift. And in '42, in-- in-- in-- that was in-- in fall, I think, my brother came to the house, my father came, and they-- my brother said, I heard something is-- he had a hint from a German that at night the ghetto will be surrounded, and they're going to take out-- liquidate, some-- some people. And I was at the time on the night shift. And my father and my brother gave me-- they told me, you-- you better go to work. You don't stay. I remember I-- I did not want to go, but my mother pushed me, pushed me out. And that's the last time I-- I saw her. I kissed her goodbye, and she said to me, don't cry. I remember like today my niece. She lives here in-- in Camarillo now. She was a baby at-- at that time. And at work, while I was at work, we supposed to already come back to the ghetto, but Schindler didn't let us go. He gave us a room there. He said, the ghetto is surrounded, you better don't go. And then I knew already that I will never see them again. That was-- that time in '42. They took my mother and my two younger sisters. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back in the morning, early in the morning, when everything was already over, they-- where I came to ghetto, and-- and I went into the place where we left and nobody was-- was there. I didn't know what to do with myself. I started to walk the streets and cry and, all of a sudden, I saw my brother. And when I saw my brother, I fainted.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I found out, well, my sister was working at Kabel, so she was OK. And my father was at work. He was OK. And just my mother went at that time and-- and my two younger sisters. But what I skipped before-- I don't know, can I go back? Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That from Wieliczka they took my sister at the time, and the whole-- the older sister what she stays to watch for the things-- she was the first one to-- to go. And that transport went out, so I think straight to the-- straight to the-- to the oven. And-- but my brother-in-law, they choosed him too-- because she was married-- they choosed him to work. He lived through the war. Because after the war, I never saw him, but when we came, after the war, to Krakow, we went to the house, and there was a janitor. She told us that he came back, and he asked or anybody is alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that time I-- that was ghetto B where we lived, so my brother found a place for us in the ghetto. And I was still-- then Schindler decided when they start liquidating ghetto, Schindler decided to build the living quarters, the barracks, in the back of the factory. And I went just for a short time to-- to Plaszow until they built-- until they built the living quarters. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was barracks just like in Plaszow, or-- but we were going-- we had a little bit better food. We were not afraid like in Plaszow that you never-- that death will come on the balcony and-- and shoot you while you working. And we just worked. I worked hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were making pots. So there was one place what they were making them from scratch, and then I was where they were painting them. So there were booths with-- with paint inside. And Polish people were coming there too, to work. And a guy was standing and-- and painting those big, sometimes big pots, sometimes little ones. And my job was to bring big boards with nails on them. While he painted, he puts them on these nails, and there were ovens, what later on, they were burning out those pots with-- with shelves all from the beginning of the factory to the end, to put in those boards with the pots to get them dry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my job was to take those boards, and like a waitress, carry them-- carry them to the shelves, and it was very heavy. And then bring back those empty boards to the station where he was painting. That was going on for-- to the end, I-- I already-- later on, when you painted, you know how a pot has a edge on the top? So I advanced, so my job was just to make the edges on them. But it was hard work, 12 hours a-- a day. Did you-- did they feed you while you were working there? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We got our soups, and-- and-- but my brother was helping me. He was in-- going from Plaszow to ghetto. And they were liquidating over there all the things what the Jewish people left. And he was helping himself instead of the Germans to take. And so he was sending me some money always, because our food was coming from Plaszow. And the Jewish policeman were always going with a wagon to bring the food to our-- we belonged really to Plaszow. And so he knew all those Jewish policeman, so he send me money. And when the Polish people were coming in, they were bringing for us some bread and butter, and sometimes a piece of meat and eggs. And being that there was like oven there and there were pots-- because that was the pot vessel-- we could even cook ourselves some things. And I had one girlfriend-- I even talked to her the other day. I just found out that she-- where she is. We were together, and we were like sisters. We cooked together. We cried together. We left together if it was time to, you know, to left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I had a-- a friend there-- I wouldn't call it a boyfriend. He didn't-- he didn't live through the-- he went to Mauthausen, and he didn't-- he didn't survive. So we stayed there till-- till what was it when we went to Auschwitz? In '42 or '43? And we worked there. And then to-- they were liquidating Plaszow. Stop right there.
